text: What is the size of IPv4 address?
subject: CN
explanation: IPv4 addresses are 32 bits (4 bytes), typically written in dotted-decimal notation.

text: Which protocol operates at the Transport layer?
subject: CN
explanation: TCP operates at Layer 4 (Transport). HTTP is Application, IP is Network, ARP is Data Link.

text: The maximum data rate of a noiseless channel with bandwidth B and L signal levels is?
subject: CN
explanation: Nyquist theorem: Max data rate = 2B log₂ L for a noiseless channel.

text: Which layer is responsible for end-to-end delivery of the entire message?
subject: CN
explanation: The Transport layer ensures complete end-to-end message delivery with error recovery and flow control.

text: ARP resolves?
subject: CN
explanation: ARP (Address Resolution Protocol) maps IP addresses to MAC addresses on a local network.

text: In TCP, a 3-way handshake uses which flags?
subject: CN
explanation: TCP connection: Client→SYN, Server→SYN-ACK, Client→ACK.

text: Subnet mask 255.255.255.224 supports how many hosts per subnet?
subject: CN
explanation: 224 = 11100000, so 5 host bits → 2⁵ - 2 = 30 usable hosts (subtract network and broadcast).

text: Which routing algorithm uses Dijkstra's algorithm?
subject: CN
explanation: OSPF uses Dijkstra's shortest-path algorithm (link-state routing). RIP uses Bellman-Ford (distance-vector).

text: In Go-Back-N ARQ with window size N, how many frames can be sent before an ACK is needed?
subject: CN
explanation: In Go-Back-N, the sender can transmit up to N frames without waiting for an acknowledgment.

text: Which protocol is connectionless?
subject: CN
explanation: UDP is connectionless — no handshake, no guaranteed delivery, lower overhead than TCP.

text: CSMA/CD is used in which type of network?
subject: CN
explanation: CSMA/CD (Carrier Sense Multiple Access/Collision Detection) is the access method for wired Ethernet networks.

text: What is the maximum payload size of an Ethernet frame?
subject: CN
explanation: Standard Ethernet MTU is 1500 bytes. The full frame (with header/trailer) is 1518 bytes.

text: DNS uses which transport layer protocol primarily for queries?
subject: CN
explanation: DNS uses UDP (port 53) for standard queries (fast, small packets). TCP is used for zone transfers and large responses.

text: What is the purpose of NAT?
options: Translate private IP addresses to public IP addresses
subject: CN
explanation: NAT maps private IPs to public IPs, conserving IPv4 addresses and providing basic security.

text: What layer does a switch operate at?
subject: CN
explanation: Switches operate at Layer 2 (Data Link), forwarding frames based on MAC addresses.

text: What layer does a router operate at?
subject: CN
explanation: Routers operate at Layer 3 (Network), forwarding packets based on IP addresses.

text: What is the purpose of DHCP?
options: Automatically assign IP addresses to devices
subject: CN
explanation: DHCP dynamically assigns IP addresses, subnet masks, gateways, and DNS servers to network devices.

text: Which protocol is used for secure web browsing?
subject: CN
explanation: HTTPS = HTTP over TLS/SSL, encrypting communication between browser and server.

text: What is the difference between IPv4 and IPv6?
subject: CN
explanation: IPv4: 32-bit (4.3B addresses). IPv6: 128-bit (3.4×10³⁸ addresses). IPv6 also simplifies headers.

text: What is a VLAN?
options: Virtual Local Area Network — logically segments a physical network
subject: CN
explanation: VLANs logically partition a physical LAN into separate broadcast domains using switch configuration.

text: What is the purpose of ICMP?
options: Send error messages and diagnostic info about IP packets
subject: CN
explanation: ICMP reports errors (destination unreachable, time exceeded) and handles diagnostic tools like ping and traceroute.

text: What is the sliding window protocol?
options: Flow control mechanism allowing multiple frames in transit
subject: CN
explanation: Sliding window protocols allow senders to transmit multiple frames before receiving an ACK, improving throughput.

text: What is multiplexing in networking?
options: Combining multiple signals on a single channel
subject: CN
explanation: Multiplexing combines multiple signals on one channel (TDM, FDM, WDM, CDM).

text: What is the difference between TCP and UDP?
options: TCP is connectionless, UDP is connection-oriented
TCP is reliable and connection-oriented, UDP is unreliable and connectionless
subject: CN
explanation: TCP: reliable, ordered, connection-oriented. UDP: unreliable, unordered, connectionless but faster.

text: What is the purpose of BGP?
options: Exchange routing information between autonomous systems
subject: CN
explanation: BGP (Border Gateway Protocol) is the inter-domain routing protocol that connects autonomous systems on the internet.

text: What is a socket in networking?
options: An endpoint defined by IP address + port number for communication
subject: CN
explanation: A socket is an IP:port pair that serves as an endpoint for network communication.

text: What is the purpose of CRC?
subject: CN
explanation: CRC (Cyclic Redundancy Check) appends check bits to data for detecting transmission errors.

text: Which protocol is used for email sending?
subject: CN
explanation: SMTP (Simple Mail Transfer Protocol) sends emails. POP3/IMAP are for receiving/reading.

text: What is the default port for HTTP?
subject: CN
explanation: HTTP uses port 80 by default. HTTPS uses 443, FTP uses 21, SMTP uses 25.

text: What is congestion control in TCP?
options: Mechanisms to prevent network overload by adjusting sending rate
subject: CN
explanation: TCP congestion control (slow start, congestion avoidance, fast retransmit/recovery) prevents network congestion.

text: What is the purpose of a firewall?
options: Filter network traffic based on security rules
subject: CN
explanation: Firewalls filter incoming/outgoing traffic based on rules — blocking unauthorized access while permitting legitimate traffic.

text: What is a MAC address?
options: A 48-bit hardware address uniquely identifying a network interface
subject: CN
explanation: MAC (Media Access Control) addresses are 48-bit hardware identifiers burned into network interface cards.

text: What is the Shannon capacity formula?
subject: CN
explanation: Shannon-Hartley: C = B log₂(1 + S/N), giving the maximum data rate for a noisy channel.

text: What is a proxy server?
options: An intermediary that handles client requests to other servers
subject: CN
explanation: Proxy servers act as intermediaries — providing caching, anonymity, filtering, and load balancing.

text: What is the purpose of the TTL field in an IP packet?
options: Limit packet lifetime to prevent infinite loops
subject: CN
explanation: TTL is decremented at each hop. When it reaches 0, the packet is discarded, preventing routing loops.

text: What is Selective Repeat ARQ?
options: Only retransmit the specific frames that were lost or errored
subject: CN
explanation: Selective Repeat retransmits only the specific frames that failed — more efficient than Go-Back-N.

text: What is the difference between hub, switch, and router?
options: Switch uses IP, router uses MAC
Hub broadcasts, switch uses MAC, router uses IP
subject: CN
explanation: Hub: L1 broadcast. Switch: L2 MAC-based forwarding. Router: L3 IP-based routing between networks.

text: What is TCP flow control?
options: Receiver advertises window size to control sender's rate
subject: CN
explanation: TCP flow control uses the receiver's advertised window to prevent the sender from overwhelming the receiver's buffer.

text: What is the size of an IPv6 address?
subject: CN
explanation: IPv6 addresses are 128 bits (16 bytes), written as eight groups of four hexadecimal digits.

text: What is a VPN?
options: Virtual Private Network — creates encrypted tunnel over public network
subject: CN
explanation: VPNs create encrypted tunnels over the public internet, providing secure private network access.

text: What is OSPF?
options: An interior gateway protocol using link-state routing
subject: CN
explanation: OSPF is a link-state IGP where routers build a complete topology map and use Dijkstra's algorithm.

text: What is the purpose of DNS caching?
options: Store previously resolved domain-to-IP mappings to reduce DNS query time
subject: CN
explanation: DNS caching stores resolved name-to-IP mappings locally to avoid repeated DNS lookups for the same domain.

text: What is a broadcast address?
options: An address that sends packets to all hosts in a subnet
The first address in a subnet
subject: CN
explanation: The broadcast address (all host bits = 1) delivers a packet to every host in the subnet.

text: What is the difference between half-duplex and full-duplex?
options: Half-duplex is one direction at a time, full-duplex is both simultaneously
subject: CN
explanation: Half-duplex: data flows one direction at a time (walkie-talkie). Full-duplex: both directions simultaneously (phone call).

text: What is CIDR notation?
options: IP address followed by /prefix_length to specify subnet mask
subject: CN
explanation: CIDR (e.g., 192.168.1.0/24) specifies IP and subnet mask length, replacing classful addressing.

text: What is the TCP slow start algorithm?
options: TCP begins with small congestion window, doubling it each RTT until threshold
TCP sends one packet at a time
TCP starts transmitting slowly and linearly increases
subject: CN
explanation: Slow start begins with cwnd=1 MSS, doubling each RTT (exponential growth) until reaching ssthresh.

text: What is an AS (Autonomous System)?
options: A collection of networks under one administrative authority with a unified routing policy
subject: CN
explanation: An AS is a set of routers/networks managed by a single organization with a consistent routing policy.

text: What is the purpose of the checksum field in TCP?
options: Verify data integrity of the segment
subject: CN
explanation: The TCP checksum verifies that the segment wasn't corrupted during transmission.

text: What is the difference between static and dynamic routing?
options: Static is only for large networks
Static is manually configured, dynamic uses protocols to automatically update routes
subject: CN
explanation: Static: admin configures routes manually. Dynamic: protocols (OSPF, RIP, BGP) automatically discover and update routes.

text: What is the purpose of the SYN flag in TCP?
options: Initiate a connection by synchronizing sequence numbers
subject: CN
explanation: SYN synchronizes sequence numbers between client and server to establish a TCP connection.

text: What is port forwarding?
options: Redirecting traffic from one IP:port to another IP:port
subject: CN
explanation: Port forwarding redirects incoming traffic on a specific port to a different host/port — common with NAT.

text: What is the OSI model?
options: A 7-layer reference model for network communication
subject: CN
explanation: OSI: Physical, Data Link, Network, Transport, Session, Presentation, Application — 7 layers abstracting network communication.

text: What is the TCP/IP model?
options: A 4-layer practical model: Link, Internet, Transport, Application
subject: CN
explanation: The TCP/IP model has 4 layers: Network Access (Link), Internet, Transport, Application.

text: What is the purpose of a load balancer?
options: Distribute network traffic across multiple servers for performance and reliability
subject: CN
explanation: Load balancers distribute incoming requests across server pools using algorithms like round-robin, least connections, or weighted.

text: What is the maximum number of hosts in a /24 network?
subject: CN
explanation: /24 = 8 host bits → 2⁸ - 2 = 254 usable hosts (minus network and broadcast addresses).

text: What is QoS (Quality of Service)?
options: Mechanisms to prioritize certain types of network traffic
subject: CN
explanation: QoS prioritizes traffic types (VoIP, video, data) to ensure performance for latency-sensitive applications.

text: What is the difference between unicast, multicast, and broadcast?
options: Unicast: one-to-one, Multicast: one-to-group, Broadcast: one-to-all
subject: CN
explanation: Unicast: single destination. Multicast: specific group. Broadcast: all hosts on subnet.

text: What is TCP Reno?
options: A TCP version that uses fast retransmit and fast recovery to handle congestion
subject: CN
explanation: TCP Reno adds fast retransmit (triple duplicate ACKs) and fast recovery, halving cwnd instead of resetting to 1.

text: What is HTTP/2's main improvement over HTTP/1.1?
options: Multiplexing multiple requests over a single TCP connection
subject: CN
explanation: HTTP/2 multiplexes requests on one TCP connection, uses header compression, and supports server push.

text: What is the purpose of RARP?
options: Resolve MAC to IP — used by diskless workstations to obtain their IP
subject: CN
explanation: RARP (Reverse ARP) maps MAC addresses to IP addresses — primarily for diskless stations. Replaced by DHCP.

text: A company's website responds normally to local users but international users experience 5-second delays. CDN is not configured. What's the primary issue and solution?
options: High latency due to geographic distance — implement a CDN with edge servers
subject: CN
explanation: Geographic distance increases RTT. A CDN caches content at edge locations near users, reducing latency.

text: A TCP connection between a client and server keeps sending data but the receiver's buffer fills up and segments are dropped. What TCP mechanism should handle this?
subject: CN
explanation: TCP flow control: receiver advertises its available buffer space (rwnd). Sender must not send more than rwnd bytes unacknowledged.

text: During a DDoS attack, a web server receives 1 million TCP SYN packets per second but no connections complete. What type of attack is this and what's the mitigation?
options: SYN flood attack — use SYN cookies or rate limiting
subject: CN
explanation: SYN flood fills the server's half-open connection table. SYN cookies generate the ISN from a hash, eliminating the need to store state.

text: A company has offices in Mumbai and Bangalore. They need 50 hosts in Mumbai and 100 in Bangalore from the 192.168.10.0/24 block. Design the subnetting.
options: Give /24 to Bangalore and /28 to Mumbai
subject: CN
explanation: VLSM: Bangalore needs 100 hosts → /25 (126 hosts), Mumbai needs 50 → /26 (62 hosts). Allocate largest first.

text: A microservices application deployed on Kubernetes has services that can't communicate across pods. Pods are scheduled on different nodes. DNS resolution within the cluster works. What's likely wrong?
options: Network policies or firewall rules blocking inter-node pod traffic
subject: CN
explanation: Kubernetes Network Policies or underlying firewall rules can block pod-to-pod traffic across nodes. Check CNI plugin config and network policies.

text: A video call application uses TCP for media streams. Users complain about freezing and audio gaps. What protocol change would improve quality?
options: Switch to UDP with RTP for media streaming
subject: CN
explanation: TCP's retransmissions cause delays unacceptable for real-time media. UDP + RTP provides low-latency streaming with tolerable packet loss.

text: An organization's DNS is returning stale IP addresses after migrating their website to a new server. The old IP still receives traffic hours after DNS records were updated. What's happening?
options: DNS caches at various levels still hold the old record (respect TTL)
subject: CN
explanation: DNS records are cached at multiple levels (browser, OS, ISP resolvers). Old records persist until their TTL expires. Solution: set low TTL before migration.

text: A REST API is served over HTTPS but a security audit reveals it's vulnerable to a man-in-the-middle attack. The server uses a self-signed certificate. What's the fix?
options: Use a CA-signed TLS certificate and implement certificate pinning
subject: CN
explanation: Self-signed certificates aren't trusted by clients — they accept any certificate. Use CA-signed certs and consider HPKP/certificate pinning.

text: A company's internal network experiences broadcast storms causing network-wide slowdown. All 500 workstations are on a single flat LAN. What's the solution?
options: Segment the network into VLANs to create separate broadcast domains
subject: CN
explanation: VLANs segment the flat network into smaller broadcast domains, containing broadcast traffic within each VLAN.

text: A WebSocket-based chat application works on localhost but fails when deployed behind an Nginx reverse proxy. HTTP works fine. What configuration is likely missing?
options: Nginx WebSocket proxy configuration (Upgrade and Connection headers)
subject: CN
explanation: Nginx must forward WebSocket upgrade headers: proxy_set_header Upgrade $http_upgrade; proxy_set_header Connection 'upgrade';

text: What is MTU path discovery?
options: Determining the maximum packet size that can traverse a path without fragmentation
subject: CN
explanation: Path MTU Discovery finds the maximum packet size by sending packets with DF bit set and reducing size on ICMP errors.

text: What is the purpose of the FIN flag in TCP?
subject: CN
explanation: FIN indicates the sender has finished sending data — initiating a graceful TCP connection termination.

text: What is ARP spoofing?
options: Attacker sends fake ARP messages to link their MAC to a legitimate IP
subject: CN
explanation: ARP spoofing sends forged ARP replies to associate the attacker's MAC with the target's IP — enabling MITM attacks.

text: What is Nagle's algorithm?
options: TCP optimization that buffers small packets to reduce overhead
subject: CN
explanation: Nagle's algorithm combines small outgoing messages and sends them together to reduce the number of TCP segments.

text: What is the difference between L4 and L7 load balancing?
options: L4 routes based on IP/port, L7 inspects application-layer content (URL, headers)
subject: CN
explanation: L4 load balancers route based on TCP/IP info. L7 can make decisions based on HTTP headers, URLs, cookies.

text: What is network segmentation?
options: Dividing a network into smaller sub-networks for security and performance
subject: CN
explanation: Segmentation isolates network segments, limiting lateral movement of attacks and reducing broadcast domains.

text: What is the TIME_WAIT state in TCP?
options: A state after FIN where the socket waits 2×MSL before fully closing
subject: CN
explanation: TIME_WAIT (2×MSL ≈ 60-120s) ensures delayed segments don't confuse future connections on the same port.

subject: CN
explanation: RARP (Reverse ARP) maps MAC addresses to IP addresses — primarily for diskless stations. Replaced by DHCP.

subject: CN

subject: CN

subject: CN

subject: CN

subject: CN

text: Which protocol is used to secure HTTP connections?
subject: CN

text: Identify the class of IP address 192.168.1.1
subject: CN

text: Which layer of the OSI model handles error detection and flow control across a single link?
subject: CN

text: What is the standard port for SMTP?
subject: CN

text: What algorithm is primarily used in Link State Routing?
subject: CN

text: IEEE 802.11 refers to which of the following?
subject: CN

text: In TCP congestion control, what happens when a timeout occurs?
options: Window size is set to 1 MSS
subject: CN

text: What is the maximum payload size of a standard Ethernet frame?
subject: CN

text: In the IPv4 header, what is the purpose of the TTL (Time to Live) field?
subject: CN

text: Which DNS record type maps a domain name to an IPv6 address?
subject: CN

text: BGP (Border Gateway Protocol) is an example of which type of routing protocol?
subject: CN

text: In the CSMA/CD protocol, what does a station do immediately after detecting a collision?
options: Stops transmitting and waits for a random time
subject: CN
